name: Business Requirements Document
description: Formal BRD following the Adani 13-section template structure with numbered headings, sub-sections, and table-based requirements.
system_instructions: You are a senior business analyst writing a formal Business Requirements Document (BRD) for an Adani enterprise project. Write in clear, professional business language. Be specific and extract concrete facts, numbers, and entities from the provided source documents. Each section should stand alone — write complete sentences, not fragments. Avoid repeating content from other sections. For table sections, always output valid markdown pipe tables with a header row and separator row.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.instructions: Write the Purpose section for this BRD. Explain WHY this document exists: (1) the business initiative or project it describes, (2) the intended audience (business stakeholders, project team, approvers), and (3) how this document should be used. Keep it concise — 2-3 short paragraphs. Extract the project name, sponsoring entity, and strategic context from the source documents.

sections.title: Scope
sections.instructions: Write the Scope section. Define exactly what this project covers and what it excludes. Structure as: (1) a brief opening paragraph describing the overall scope, (2) an **In Scope** bullet list of included functional areas, modules, processes, and integrations, (3) an **Out of Scope** bullet list of explicit exclusions to prevent scope creep. Be specific — reference actual systems, teams, or process names from the source documents.

sections.title: Terms and Definition
sections.instructions:
Create a Terms and Definitions table. Extract all domain-specific terms, acronyms, abbreviations, and technical phrases from the source documents. Output as a markdown pipe table with exactly two columns:

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| ... | ... |

Include 10-20 terms. Write definitions in plain English — no jargon in the definition of a jargon term.

sections.title: Business need and why?
sections.instructions: Write the 'Business need and why?' sub-section (under Business Requirements Summary). Explain the core business problem or opportunity that is driving this project. Answer: What gap or pain point exists today? Why does the business need this solution now? What will happen if nothing is done? Draw specific facts, numbers, and business context from the source documents. Write 2-3 focused paragraphs.

sections.title: Context for business need
sections.instructions: Write the 'Context for business need' sub-section. Provide background context: the industry environment, regulatory landscape, strategic direction, or market forces that make this initiative necessary. Link the business need to broader organisational goals. Reference specific market conditions, government mandates, or Adani strategic priorities mentioned in the source documents. Write 2-3 paragraphs.

sections.title: Problem statement
sections.instructions: Write a clear Problem Statement. This should be a precise, factual description of the current-state problem — not the solution. Structure: (1) what the current situation is, (2) the specific pain points or inefficiencies (use numbers or percentages if available), (3) the consequence of not solving this problem (financial, operational, compliance risk). Keep focused — 1-2 paragraphs maximum.

sections.title: Business value delivered
sections.instructions:
Write the 'Business value delivered' sub-section. Describe the tangible and intangible business benefits this solution will deliver. Start with a brief paragraph summarising the overall value proposition, then list specific benefits as bullets:
- Quantitative benefits (cost savings, time reduction, throughput increase)
- Qualitative benefits (improved visibility, regulatory compliance, risk reduction)
- Strategic benefits (competitive advantage, scalability, new capabilities)
Extract any ROI figures, efficiency metrics, or benefit estimates from the source documents.

sections.title: Competitor landscape
sections.instructions:
Write the 'Competitor landscape' sub-section. List existing solutions or competitors in the market that address a similar problem. For each, provide a brief description of what they offer and any key differentiators. Format as bullets:
- **[Competitor/Solution Name]**: brief description of their approach
If source documents mention specific vendors or alternative approaches, reference them. Include 4-8 entries. If no competitor info is in the source, describe the general market landscape for this type of solution.

sections.title: Similar digital solutions
sections.instructions:
Write the 'Similar digital solutions' sub-section. List digital/technology solutions (platforms, tools, frameworks) that are similar to or could be considered for this project. For each, briefly note what it does and why it may or may not fit. Format as bullets:
- **[Solution Name]**: what it is and relevance to this project
Draw from any technology references in the source documents. Include 3-6 entries.

sections.title: Positioning
sections.instructions: Write the 'Positioning' sub-section. Explain how the proposed solution is positioned relative to competitors and similar solutions. What makes this approach unique or better suited to the organisation's needs? Cover: differentiation factors, fit with Adani's technology strategy, and why this solution is preferred over alternatives. Write 1-2 paragraphs.

sections.title: Critical success factors
sections.instructions:
Write the 'Critical success factors' sub-section. List the 5-8 most critical factors that must be in place for this project to succeed. These are not requirements — they are pre-conditions, enablers, or environmental factors. Format as a bullet list where each item starts with a bold title followed by a colon and explanation:
- **[Factor Name]**: why it is critical and what it means in practice
Examples: executive sponsorship, data quality, user adoption, integration readiness.

sections.title: Pain areas or opportunities in existing operational process
sections.instructions:
Identify pain areas and improvement opportunities in the OPERATIONAL processes that this project addresses. Focus on human/business process inefficiencies — not technology issues. Format as a bullet list where each item starts with a bold label:
- **[Pain Area Name]**: specific description of the operational problem, its frequency or scale, and its business impact
Extract specific process names, teams, or workflows from the source documents. Include 5-8 pain areas.

sections.title: Pain areas or opportunities in existing technology/systems
sections.instructions:
Identify pain areas and improvement opportunities in the TECHNOLOGY and SYSTEMS that this project addresses. Focus on system limitations, integration gaps, data quality issues, and technical debt. Format as a bullet list where each item starts with a bold label:
- **[Technology Pain Point]**: what the limitation is, which system or integration it affects, and the business consequence
Extract specific system names, tools, or platforms from the source documents. Include 4-7 pain areas.

sections.title: Business functionality impact
sections.instructions: Write the 'Business functionality impact' sub-section. Describe which business functions and departments will be affected by this project — and how. Cover both the areas that will directly use the new solution and areas impacted indirectly (e.g., finance, compliance, operations, field teams). Write 2-3 paragraphs discussing the nature and degree of the impact.

sections.title: As-Is Business Process
sections.instructions: Describe the As-Is (current) business process that this project will change. Walk through the current process step by step: who does what, what systems are used, where manual work happens, where data is captured, and where handoffs occur between teams. Highlight the most inefficient or error-prone steps. Write as a narrative description — do NOT use a process table format. 2-4 paragraphs.

sections.title: Business use cases
sections.instructions:
Create a Business Use Cases table listing all key business use cases for this solution. Output as a markdown pipe table with these exact columns:

| Use Case # | Use Case Name | Trigger Event | Primary Actor | Description |
|------------|--------------|--------------|---------------|-------------|
| UC-01 | ... | ... | ... | ... |

Include 8-15 use cases. Trigger Event = what starts this use case. Primary Actor = the role or system that initiates it. Description = 1-2 sentence explanation of what happens and the expected outcome. Extract from source documents — use actual business activities described there.

sections.title: Business requirements
sections.instructions:
Create a Business Requirements table. These are high-level WHAT the business needs — not HOW the system will do it (that goes in functional requirements). Output as a markdown pipe table:

| Req ID | Business Requirement | Business Value | Priority |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|----------|
| BR-001 | ... | ... | Must Have |

Priority values: Must Have / Should Have / Could Have / Won't Have (MoSCoW). Include 10-18 business requirements grouped by theme. Each requirement should be a single clear statement of business need.

sections.title: Functional requirements
sections.instructions:
Create a Functional Requirements table. These describe HOW the system must behave — specific capabilities the solution must provide. Output as a markdown pipe table:

| Req # | Functional Requirement | Phase | Description |
|-------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| FR-001 | ... | Phase 1 | ... |

Phase values: Phase 1 (MVP) / Phase 2 / Phase 3. Include 15-25 functional requirements. Each 'Functional Requirement' cell should start with 'The system SHALL...' or 'The system MUST...'. Description provides detail and business justification. Extract from source documents — map each described feature to a clear FR.

sections.title: Non-functional requirements
sections.instructions:
Create a Non-Functional Requirements table covering quality attributes. Output as a markdown pipe table:

| NFR # | Category | Phase | Description | Rationale |
|-------|----------|-------|-------------|----------|
| NFR-001 | Performance | Phase 1 | ... | ... |

Categories to include: Performance, Availability, Security, Scalability, Usability, Integration, Compliance, Data Retention, Disaster Recovery. Include 12-20 NFRs. Each Description must be specific and measurable (e.g., 'API response time < 2 seconds for 95th percentile under 500 concurrent users'). Rationale explains the business reason for the constraint.

sections.title: Solution overview-Cloud first approach
sections.instructions: Write the 'Solution Overview – Cloud First Approach' section. Describe the proposed solution architecture and approach. Cover: (1) a paragraph summarising the overall solution concept and technology approach (cloud-first, microservices, API-driven etc.), (2) bullet list of key technical components or modules, (3) a paragraph on the cloud deployment strategy (which cloud platform, deployment model, data residency). Reference any specific technologies, platforms, or architectural patterns mentioned in the source documents.

sections.title: Key constraints
sections.instructions:
List the key constraints that the project team must operate within. These are fixed limits — not risks or assumptions. Format as bullets grouped by type:

**Budget Constraints**
- ...

**Timeline Constraints**
- ...

**Technical Constraints**
- ...

**Regulatory / Compliance Constraints**
- ...

**Organisational Constraints**
- ...

Extract specific constraints from the source documents. Include 2-3 bullets per category.

sections.title: Project schedule
sections.instructions:
Create a Project Schedule / Milestone table. Output as a markdown pipe table:

| S.N. | Project Milestone | Time Duration |
|------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | ... | ... weeks / Month YYYY |

Include 6-12 milestones covering: project kickoff, requirements sign-off, design, development phases, testing (SIT/UAT), go-live, and hypercare. If the source document mentions specific timelines, dates, or phases, use them. Otherwise create a realistic timeline for this type of project. Time Duration should be specific (e.g., '4 weeks', 'Month 3', 'Q2 2025').

sections.title: Other constraints and assumptions if any
sections.instructions:
Create an Other Constraints and Assumptions table. This captures additional assumptions (things believed true but unconfirmed) and constraints not covered elsewhere. Output as a markdown pipe table:

| Sr. No. | Topic | Comments |
|---------|-------|----------|
| 1 | ... | ... |

Include 8-15 entries covering: data migration assumptions, third-party dependency assumptions, user training assumptions, infrastructure assumptions, regulatory assumptions, vendor assumptions, and any project-specific constraints or dependencies. Be specific — each Comment should be a clear statement.
